intro: The same 3D scene as in the workshop, now as a real WebXR session. Hit “Enter AR” on a Quest 3 or Galaxy XR (or Chrome with the WebXR emulator) — the board appears at bench height in your room.

fallback.title: No XR device available.
fallback.body: You can still use the demo in 3D — in the <link>workshop</link>. For XR you need a Galaxy XR, a Quest 3 or Chrome with the WebXR emulator (chrome://flags/#webxr-incubations).

centerTitle: Bring board and view back to center
preview: Preview shows the current learning step: {step} · board {width}×{length}mm, {pins} pins, 1:{ratio}. Adjustable in the workshop (state shared via localStorage).

anreissen.name: Lay out
anreissen.hint: Transfer the layout lines onto the wood — the foundation for everything.

saegen.name: Saw
saegen.hint: Saw on the waste side, leave the layout line standing.

stemmen.name: Chop
stemmen.hint: Chop out the waste down to the baseline — from both sides.

passen.name: Fit
passen.hint: Mark the mating piece and dry-fit it.

pruefen.name: Check
pruefen.hint: Check the seams and refine until the joint closes up snug.

detail.messen: Before we do any math, we measure the board carefully. B is the width ({B} mm, edge to edge), D the thickness ({D} mm, how thick the board is). These two measurements determine the whole dovetail layout — that's all we need. Careful measuring is the foundation: one wrong number runs through the entire calculation.
detail.schwalbenzahl: AZS is the number of tails. Formula: AZS = B / (1.7 * D). Why 1.7? It's a rule of thumb from the workshop — it gives a pleasing ratio of tail to board thickness (not too many tiny ones, not too few chunky ones). Here: {B} / (1.7 * {D}) = {B} / {BD} = {AZSraw} ~ {AZS} tails. If you want fewer, wider tails, use 2 instead of 1.7.
detail.teile: AZT is the number of layout divisions. A tail is 2 parts wide, a pin 1 part — so 3 parts per tail, plus 1 for the two half pins: AZT = AZS * 3 + 1 = {AZS} * 3 + 1 = {AZT}. The division width T = B / AZT = {B} / {AZT} = {T} mm. That makes each pin {zinken} mm and each tail {schwalbe} mm wide.
detail.streichmass: With the marking gauge we scribe the board thickness D = {D} mm all the way around the board. This line is the chopping depth — you chop down to here, never deeper. It runs the same on all four sides, so the bottom of the joint sits cleanly at one level.
detail.markieren: Now we divide the width into the {AZT} calculated parts and mark the {AZS} tails. Pins narrow (1 part, {zinken} mm), tails wide (2 parts, {schwalbe} mm). Step off the division points cleanly with dividers or a ruler — evenly spaced gives that calm, handsome look.
detail.schraege: The tail flank gets a slope of 1 : {ratio} (about {deg} degrees). Set each flank with the sliding bevel. Steeper, and the tips break out (that's short end grain there); shallower, and it holds worse under pull. 1 : {ratio} is the proven middle ground between grip and durability.
detail.fertig: The layout is done: {AZS} tails in {AZT} parts, each {T} mm, with a slope of 1 : {ratio}. Now comes the sawing — always on the waste side of the line, so the layout line stays as a fine mark. Later it's your reference when fitting.
